question: Explain the purpose of using comments in Java code. Provide two examples of different comment types and describe when you would use each.
expectedPoints: Comments are non-executable statements used to document code, making it easier for developers to understand its purpose, logic, and functionality.
Single-line comments (//) are used for brief explanations on a single line.
Multi-line comments (/* ... */) are used for longer explanations that span multiple lines or to temporarily disable blocks of code.

question: Compare and contrast single-line comments, multi-line comments, and JavaDoc comments. Explain the primary use case for JavaDoc comments and what makes them unique.
expectedPoints: Single-line comments begin with // and are for short, inline explanations.
Multi-line comments are enclosed in /* */ and can span multiple lines for more detailed notes or code disabling.
JavaDoc comments begin with /** */ and are used to generate formal API documentation automatically.
JavaDoc comments are unique because they contain special tags (e.g., @param, @return, @author) that describe the code's API elements for users.

question: Critically evaluate the statement: 'Well-written code doesn't need comments.' Discuss the role of comments in professional software development, including best practices for writing effective comments and potential pitfalls of over-commenting or writing poor comments.
expectedPoints: While self-documenting code (clear naming, good structure) is ideal, comments are crucial for explaining the 'why' behind complex logic, algorithms, or business rules that aren't obvious from the code itself.
Best practices include: commenting non-trivial code sections, using JavaDoc for public APIs, keeping comments concise and up-to-date, and explaining intent rather than just restating the code.
Pitfalls include: redundant comments that state the obvious, misleading comments that are not updated with code changes, and excessive commenting that clutters the code and reduces readability.
Comments are a key part of maintainability, onboarding new developers, and future-proofing the codebase, making them essential in professional development despite the quality of the code.
